text: Surgery of the pancreas remains one of the most talented fields in general surgery.
There is only scarce data on robotic approach of pancreatic lesions.
Tumors of pancreatic tail can be resected laparoscopically with good results.
Robotics may offer a more stable platform for dissection and mobilization of such tumors, especially when the spleen should be preserved.
Whipple pancreatodermatoma is a long and challenging procedure which may be facilitated from robotics.
We present here our experience with robotic pancreatic cases including six patients that underwent distal pancreatectomy as in the video already showing and two of them with spleen preservation.
In one case There was a large pancreatic cyst removal and two cases that underwent the WIPI pancretodermectomy using a totally robotic technique.

Based on an extensive experience with paroscoping and robotic operations and after a series of successful digital pancreatectomies and hybrid pancretodermectomies, we established a totally robotic technique for WIPI pancretodermectomy.
The surgeon performs advanced dissection near the splenic hilum and above the pancreas, with visible separation of tissue layers in the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies the major splenic trunks, which are visible in the surgical field.
We usually, we always use the technique of recognizing each vessel separately and controlling them early in the operation by using vessel loops.
We find the use of da Vinci instruments extremely useful for fine dissection around the vessels without any risk of traumatizing the vessels and avoiding the risk of bleeding.
Especially in attempts for protecting the spleen, controlling the vessels is of increased importance.
The surgeon has dissected the pancreatic tail free from surrounding tissues, creating a visible plane to maneuver it over the vessels for subsequent division.
The surgeon completes the final detachment of the pancreatic tail from the splenic hilum, ensuring the separation of these anatomical structures.
The surgeon uses a linear stapler to divide the pancreatic tail, including the mass within it, completing the separation from the surrounding structures.
This mass eventually was found to be a benign ectopic splenic mass.
The patient had an uneventful recovery and was discharged a few days after surgery without any complications.
The surgeon places the dissected specimen, including the pancreatic tail, into an ento bag for removal from the surgical field.
The surgeon extends the Kocher maneuver by lifting the duodenum, providing better exposure of the surgical field.

The surgeon turns the surgical system towards the right abdomen, focusing on the root of the mesentery. The fore portion of the duodenum and the duodenal jejunal junction are visible as the jejunum is being freed during the Whipple procedure.
The surgical specimen is nearly prepared for removal, with visible final adjustments being made to ensure complete detachment from surrounding tissues.
For the first patient, the total operative time was approximately nine hours. Patient received clear liquids on postoperative day four and was discharged on day ten.
Histology showed a ductal adenocarcinoma of the pancreatic head with six of fifteen peripancreatic lymph nodes involved. The stage was T-III-N-I.

For the second patient, total operative time was approximately seven hours. The patient received clear liquids on day six and was discharged on day twelve. Histology revealed a ductal adenocarcinoma and invasion of one out of ten regional lymph nodes. The stage was T-III-N-I.
In both patients, the robotic platform proved useful during dissection near the SMV and during construction of the anastomosis.

Robotic assistance in pancreatic surgery may overcome the limitations of laparoscopy with potentially lower blood loss and morbidity. Of course, further randomized trials and longer follow-up will be necessary to validate these results.

The surgeon divides the common bile duct and locates the stent, followed by the removal of the stent. The cholecystectomy is completed, and the specimen is removed through a small extension of the right upper robotic incision.

The surgeon regains pneumoperitoneum by suturing the incision, followed by redocking the robotic system. The continuity of the gastrointestinal tract is repaired through the visible threonostomosis procedure.
The surgeon completes the pancreaticogastric anastomosis and the duodenojejunal anastomosis, visually connecting the pancreatic tissue to the stomach and the duodenum to the jejunum.
The surgeon performs an anastomosis using a linear stapler, followed by suturing the defect with robotic needle holders.
As everybody knows robotic system is ideal for such fine suturing in narrow spaces.
The surgeon creates the biliodigestive anastomosis, connecting the bile duct to the digestive tract.
The robotic hook is used to carefully manipulate tissues, showcasing the final result of the pancreaticoduodenectomy with completed anastomoses and restored continuity of the gastrointestinal tract.
Thank you for your attention.
Although our presentation includes use of the DaVinci SI system, we have no disclosures to make. Thank you very much.
